title: Full Table Scan
context: Large table with millions of rows, no WHERE clause, no filtering, full table scan, no index used, fetches every single row
problem: Full table scan on unfiltered large table
root_cause: No WHERE clause means the database engine reads every single row in the table. With millions of records, this is catastrophic. No index can help when there is no filter condition.
suggestion: Add WHERE clause to filter rows. Add index on commonly filtered columns. Use LIMIT for pagination. Use SELECT with specific columns instead of SELECT *.
symptoms: query runs fine on dev with 1000 rows but times out on production with millions
EXPLAIN shows full table scan with rows_examined equal to total table size
query gets slower every month as data grows
database CPU spikes to 100% when this query runs
other queries slow down when this runs due to resource contention
real_world_context: Common in insurance platforms when developers run ad-hoc reports on policy_data or claims_data without filters. A single analyst running this query can bring down the production database for all users.
detection_signals: EXPLAIN shows type=ALL, rows=millions, no key used
estimated_rows_examined: entire table — could be 10M to 100M rows
fix_validation: After adding WHERE clause and index, EXPLAIN should show type=ref or type=range with rows examined reduced by 99%

title: JSON Field Filtering
context: No index on JSON field, large dataset, JSON_EXTRACT in WHERE clause, MySQL cannot use index for JSON function in WHERE
problem: Unindexed JSON extraction in WHERE clause causes full table scan
root_cause: JSON_EXTRACT() is a function call on a column. MySQL cannot use a regular B-tree index on a function result. So even if policy_data has an index on the data column, it is bypassed entirely. Every row is read and the JSON is parsed in memory to evaluate the condition.
suggestion: Create a generated column: ALTER TABLE policy_data ADD COLUMN policy_state VARCHAR(2) GENERATED ALWAYS AS (JSON_EXTRACT(data, '$.policy.state')) STORED. Then add index on policy_state. Avoid JSON_EXTRACT in WHERE clause.
symptoms: query takes 25 seconds on production but is instant on small test datasets
EXPLAIN shows full table scan even though columns appear indexed
performance gets worse as the policy_data table grows
query is fine in development where the table has only hundreds of rows
adding a regular index on the data column does not help
real_world_context: Extremely common in Solartis-style insurance platforms where policy attributes like state, premium, and status are stored as flexible JSON documents. Filtering by policy state for regulatory reporting is a daily operation that becomes a bottleneck without generated columns.
detection_signals: EXPLAIN shows type=ALL, Extra=Using where, no key used despite index existing
estimated_rows_examined: all rows in policy_data regardless of how many match
fix_validation: After adding generated column and index, EXPLAIN should show type=ref, key=idx_policy_state, rows examined drops to only matching rows

title: High Frequency Config Query
frequency: very high - thousands of times per minute
context: Fast query individually but hammers database repeatedly with no caching, config data is static and rarely changes, every API request triggers this lookup
problem: High frequency repeated lookup on static data with no caching layer
root_cause: Each request to the application triggers a fresh database query for configuration data that almost never changes. With thousands of requests per minute, this creates unnecessary DB load. The data is the same on every call but the database processes each one independently.
suggestion: Cache config_table results in Redis with 5 minute TTL. At application startup, load entire config_table into memory. Use cache-aside pattern: check cache first, fall back to DB only on miss. Eliminates 90% of DB load for config lookups.
symptoms: database connection pool exhausted during peak traffic
config_table appears in slow query log thousands of times per hour
individual query is fast but aggregate DB load is high
removing config lookups from profiler dramatically reduces DB CPU
query count on config_table is disproportionately high compared to business operations
real_world_context: Insurance platforms like Solartis use configuration-driven systems where product rules, rate tables, and workflow configurations are stored in database tables. Every policy calculation hits these tables. Without caching, a burst of policy quotes can overwhelm the database.
detection_signals: High query count on config_table in slow query log, low individual execution time but high cumulative time
estimated_rows_examined: small per query but multiplied by thousands of calls per minute
fix_validation: After Redis caching, config_table queries should drop by 90% in DB monitoring. Cache hit rate should exceed 95%.

title: Complex Join with JSON Processing
query: SELECT * FROM policy_data p JOIN claims_data c ON p.policy_id = c.policy_id JOIN config_table cfg ON JSON_TABLE(cfg.data, '$.rules[*]' COLUMNS(rule_id INT PATH '$.id')) AS rules WHERE rules.rule_id = p.rule_id
context: Multiple table joins combined with JSON_TABLE extraction, join keys may lack indexes, JSON_TABLE creates a derived table on every execution
problem: Expensive join chain with JSON_TABLE processing creating derived tables on every query execution
root_cause: JSON_TABLE() materializes a new derived table from JSON data on every single execution. Joining this derived table with other large tables multiplies the cost. If join keys are not indexed, nested loop joins scan entire tables for each row in the outer table.
suggestion: Denormalize JSON fields into proper relational columns. Add indexes on all join keys: policy_id, claim_id, rule_id. Consider pre-materializing the JSON_TABLE result into a permanent table updated on config change. Use materialized views for complex joins that are queried frequently.
symptoms: query takes 8 seconds but only returns a few hundred rows
EXPLAIN shows derived table with full scans
performance degrades sharply when claims_data or policy_data grows
query is used in a report that runs every hour and causes noticeable slowdown
adding more joins to the query makes it exponentially slower
real_world_context: Insurance policy administration involves complex relationships between policies, claims, and business rules. When business rules are stored as JSON and joined at query time, report generation becomes a bottleneck. Solartis-style platforms commonly face this when evolving from flexible JSON schemas toward performance-optimized relational structures.
detection_signals: EXPLAIN shows derived table, multiple full scans, high rows examined relative to rows returned
estimated_rows_examined: product of all joined table sizes — can reach hundreds of millions
fix_validation: After denormalization and indexing, EXPLAIN should show index lookups on all join conditions, rows examined drops to near rows returned

title: Anomaly - Sudden Latency Spike
execution_time: spiked from 1 second to 50 seconds suddenly with no code changes
context: Previously fast query that suddenly degraded with no code changes, no schema changes, same data volume approximately, possible table bloat or lock contention
problem: Anomaly detected — sudden 50x latency spike on previously healthy query
root_cause: Sudden degradation without code changes points to infrastructure causes: table bloat from accumulated dead tuples (PostgreSQL) or fragmented pages (MySQL), lock contention from a long-running transaction blocking reads, index corruption or statistics becoming stale causing optimizer to choose wrong execution plan, or autovacuum/auto-analyze not running due to configuration issues.
suggestion: Immediately: check pg_locks or SHOW PROCESSLIST for blocking queries. Run ANALYZE on the table to refresh statistics. If MySQL, run OPTIMIZE TABLE during low traffic window. Check autovacuum logs. Monitor for lock contention patterns. Long term: implement query timeout alerts, set up automated table maintenance jobs.
symptoms: query was running in 1 second yesterday and now takes 50 seconds
no deployments or code changes were made
the issue appeared suddenly, not gradually
other queries on the same table are also slower than usual
the problem sometimes resolves itself temporarily then returns
issue is worse during business hours when transaction volume is high
real_world_context: In insurance platforms processing hundreds of policy transactions per hour, tables accumulate dead tuples quickly. If autovacuum is not tuned for high-write tables like policy_data, table bloat causes the query planner to make poor decisions. A sudden spike during business hours often indicates a long-running batch job holding locks.
detection_signals: Sudden latency increase with no code change, pg_stat_user_tables showing high n_dead_tup, SHOW PROCESSLIST showing blocked queries
estimated_rows_examined: same as before but with degraded index performance due to bloat or stale stats
fix_validation: After ANALYZE and VACUUM, query should return to baseline 1 second execution time. Monitor pg_stat_bgwriter for autovacuum activity.

title: Nested Subquery
query: SELECT * FROM policy_data WHERE policy_id IN (SELECT policy_id FROM claims_data WHERE claim_amount > 5000)
context: Subquery inside WHERE IN clause, not correlated but optimizer may execute it repeatedly, large claims_data table, missing indexes on claim_amount and policy_id
problem: Inefficient nested subquery causing repeated inner query execution and large intermediate result sets
root_cause: WHERE IN with a subquery can cause the inner query to execute for every row in the outer query depending on the optimizer version. Even when optimized, the subquery produces a large intermediate result set that must be loaded into memory. Missing indexes on claim_amount and policy_id in claims_data force full table scans.
suggestion: Rewrite as JOIN: SELECT p.* FROM policy_data p INNER JOIN claims_data c ON p.policy_id = c.policy_id WHERE c.claim_amount > 5000. Add index on claims_data(claim_amount, policy_id) as a composite index. This eliminates the subquery and lets the optimizer use hash join or index nested loop join efficiently.
symptoms: query with IN subquery takes 45 seconds but the equivalent JOIN takes 2 seconds
EXPLAIN shows dependent subquery or subquery executed millions of times
query is used in a business report that used to be fast when data was smaller
rewriting the query manually as a JOIN fixes the problem immediately
adding LIMIT to the subquery does not help
real_world_context: Insurance claims analysis frequently involves finding policies that meet certain claim criteria. Junior developers naturally write this as a subquery. In Solartis-style platforms with millions of policies and claims, this pattern becomes a critical bottleneck in reporting queries.
detection_signals: EXPLAIN shows dependent subquery or SUBQUERY in Extra column, high rows examined in inner query
estimated_rows_examined: outer table rows multiplied by inner table rows in worst case
fix_validation: After JOIN rewrite and indexing, EXPLAIN shows hash join or index nested loop, execution time drops from 45 seconds to under 2 seconds

title: Aggregation with Multiple Joins
query: SELECT state, COUNT(*) as policy_count, SUM(claim_amount) as total_claims FROM claims_data c JOIN policy_data p ON c.policy_id = p.policy_id JOIN config_table cfg ON p.product_id = cfg.product_id GROUP BY state
context: COUNT and SUM aggregation across multiple joined large tables, GROUP BY on non-indexed column, full dataset must be processed before grouping
problem: Heavy aggregation across large joined dataset without pre-aggregation or summary tables
root_cause: Aggregation functions like COUNT and SUM require processing every qualifying row before returning results. When combined with multiple joins, the intermediate result set can be enormous. GROUP BY on state requires sorting or hashing the entire joined dataset. Without indexes on join keys and the GROUP BY column, all three tables are fully scanned.
suggestion: Add composite index on policy_data(product_id, state). Add index on claims_data(policy_id, claim_amount). Consider creating a nightly pre-aggregated summary table: policy_claims_summary(state, policy_count, total_claims, report_date). For real-time needs, use materialized views refreshed every hour.
symptoms: dashboard report takes 12 seconds to load
query time increases as claims_data and policy_data grow
multiple users running the same report simultaneously causes DB slowdown
EXPLAIN shows filesort and temporary table usage
report was fast last year but gets slower each quarter
real_world_context: Insurance dashboards showing claims by state, premium totals, and policy counts are core business reporting. In Solartis platforms, these run every time a manager opens the dashboard. Without pre-aggregation, each dashboard load reruns the full join and aggregation against millions of records.
detection_signals: EXPLAIN shows Using temporary, Using filesort, high rows examined relative to result rows
estimated_rows_examined: full join of claims_data and policy_data before grouping
fix_validation: After adding summary table, dashboard query runs against pre-aggregated rows instead of millions of raw records, dropping from 12 seconds to under 100ms

title: Large Logging Table Query
context: Querying billions of rows in logging table with no partitioning, logs grow indefinitely, created_date index may exist but table size overwhelms it, no archiving strategy
problem: Full scan on massive unpartitioned logging table that has grown to billions of rows
root_cause: Logging tables are append-only and grow without bound. Even with an index on created_date, when a date range covers millions of rows the index scan is slower than expected because each index entry requires a random disk read to fetch the full row. Without partitioning, the entire table must be considered by the query planner.
suggestion: Partition table by month using MySQL RANGE partitioning on created_date. Archive logs older than 90 days to cold storage or a separate archive database. Add index on created_date within each partition. Use pt-archiver or a scheduled job for automated archiving. Query specific partition directly when possible.
symptoms: query against system_logs takes 3 minutes for a single day of data
system_logs table size is measured in hundreds of gigabytes
log analysis queries cause disk I/O spikes visible in monitoring
the table was fast 2 years ago but has grown uncontrollably
DELETing old logs to reclaim space causes table lock for hours
real_world_context: Insurance platforms generate extensive audit logs for regulatory compliance — every policy change, claim update, and user action must be logged. System_logs tables can accumulate billions of rows over years. Without a partitioning and archiving strategy from day one, the table becomes unmanageable.
detection_signals: Table size in hundreds of GB, EXPLAIN shows all partitions scanned or type=ALL despite index, high disk I/O during query
estimated_rows_examined: billions of rows even for recent date ranges
fix_validation: After partitioning, queries hitting a specific month partition examine only that month's rows. Query time drops from 180 seconds to under 1 second for single-month queries.

title: Update Query Performance
query: UPDATE policy_data SET status = 'EXPIRED' WHERE end_date < NOW()
context: Bulk update on large table, no index on end_date column, table lock blocks concurrent reads during update, runs as daily batch job
problem: Bulk update causing full table scan and table lock that blocks all concurrent reads for 60 seconds
root_cause: Without an index on end_date, MySQL must scan every row to find policies where end_date < NOW(). The UPDATE then acquires row locks or a table lock depending on the storage engine and isolation level. For 60 seconds, all SELECT queries on policy_data are blocked, causing timeout errors for application users.
suggestion: Add index on policy_data(end_date, status) as composite index. Batch the update in chunks: UPDATE policy_data SET status = EXPIRED WHERE end_date < NOW() AND status != EXPIRED LIMIT 1000. Sleep 100ms between batches to allow other queries through. Schedule during off-peak hours. Consider using pt-online-schema-change for large updates.
symptoms: application shows timeout errors every night during the batch window
customer complaints about slow response during the update period
monitoring shows database lock wait time spiking at the same time each night
the batch job ran in 5 seconds last year but now takes 60 seconds
reads on policy_data fail with lock timeout during the update
real_world_context: Insurance platforms expire policies daily based on end_date. As policy_data grows to millions of records, the nightly expiration job becomes a blocking operation. In Solartis systems processing thousands of policies per day, this daily lock causes application-wide slowdown visible to customers.
detection_signals: SHOW PROCESSLIST showing long-running UPDATE, lock wait timeouts in application logs, high Lock_time in slow query log
estimated_rows_examined: entire policy_data table to find expired policies
fix_validation: After indexing and chunking, each batch UPDATE touches only 1000 rows, runs in milliseconds, and releases locks between chunks. Total job time drops from 60 seconds to under 10 seconds with no application impact.

title: Knowledge Base Config Lookup
query: SELECT * FROM knowledge_base WHERE category = ? AND key = ?
frequency: very high — every API request triggers this
context: Configuration-driven insurance system, knowledge base queried on every single API request, no composite index on category and key, table has grown large with thousands of config entries
problem: Repeated knowledge base lookup on every request with no composite index and no caching, becoming the single biggest bottleneck in the system
root_cause: A composite WHERE clause on two columns requires a composite index covering both columns in the correct order. An index on category alone helps only partially. An index on key alone may not be used at all. Without the composite index (category, key), MySQL scans all rows matching category and then filters by key in memory. At very high frequency, even a 3-second query becomes catastrophic.
suggestion: Add composite index: CREATE INDEX idx_category_key ON knowledge_base(category, key). Cache the entire knowledge_base table in application memory at startup since config data rarely changes. Use a two-level cache: L1 in-memory HashMap for hot keys, L2 Redis for full dataset. Implement cache invalidation webhook when knowledge_base is updated.
symptoms: every API endpoint is slow even though the business logic is simple
profiling shows knowledge_base query is called on every single request
database connection pool is exhausted during peak traffic
adding more application servers makes the database problem worse
the query appears thousands of times per minute in slow query log
real_world_context: Solartis policy administration platforms are heavily configuration-driven — product rules, rate tables, validation logic, and workflow steps are all stored in knowledge base tables. Every policy quote, validation, or calculation reads from this table. Without caching, scaling the application tier makes the database bottleneck worse, not better.
detection_signals: High query count on knowledge_base table in performance schema, query appears in every request trace, database CPU high despite simple queries
estimated_rows_examined: all rows in category group before filtering by key — potentially thousands per query
fix_validation: After composite index, query drops from 3 seconds to under 10ms. After application-level caching, knowledge_base queries disappear from database monitoring entirely.
